unit.title: Wer bin ich?
unit.sub: Talk about yourself — where you're from, where you live, and what languages you speak.

steps.title: Where Are You From? 🌍
steps.desc: Germans love to know where you're from — it tells them which dialect to expect! Learn to talk about your origin and nationality.

steps.note: 'Aus' = from/out of. This is THE standard way to state your origin. 'Kommen' follows regular conjugation: ich komme, du kommst, er/sie kommt.

steps.exampleEn: Hi! Where do you come from?
steps.note: 'Woher' = from where. In formal: 'Woher kommen Sie?' Notice the verb is in position 2 — always!
A neuter noun (das). Plural: die Länder. Same root as Dutch 'het land' and English 'land'!

steps.title: 🌐 Countries in German
steps.text:
Most country names are easy to recognize:

🇩🇪 Deutschland — Germany
🇦🇹 Österreich — Austria
🇨🇭 die Schweiz — Switzerland
🇫🇷 Frankreich — France
🇬🇧 England — England
🇺🇸 Amerika / die USA — America
🇪🇸 Spanien — Spain
🇮🇹 Italien — Italy
🇹🇷 die Türkei — Turkey
🇳🇱 die Niederlande — The Netherlands

⚠️ Some countries need an article:
die Schweiz, die Türkei, die USA, die Niederlande
'Ich komme aus DER Schweiz.' (dative!)

steps.q: How do you say 'I come from France'?

steps.note: Nationalities in German are NOUNS (not adjectives like Dutch!). Male: Deutscher, Female: Deutsche. 'Ich bin Deutscher' vs 'Ich bin Deutsche'.

steps.title: 🏳️ Nationalities in German
steps.text:
German nationalities are nouns with GENDER forms:

🇩🇪 Deutscher / Deutsche — German (m/f)
🇫🇷 Franzose / Französin — French (m/f)
🇬🇧 Engländer / Engländerin — English (m/f)
🇪🇸 Spanier / Spanierin — Spanish (m/f)
🇹🇷 Türke / Türkin — Turkish (m/f)
🇺🇸 Amerikaner / Amerikanerin — American (m/f)

Examples:
• Ich bin Deutscher. — I am German. (male)
• Sie ist Französin. — She is French.
• Er ist Engländer. — He is English.

For LANGUAGES, use the adjective form:
• Ich spreche Deutsch. — I speak German.
• Er spricht Französisch. — He speaks French.

deepDive.title: Nationality ≠ Language in German
deepDive.text:
Unlike Dutch (where 'Duits' = both German nationality AND language), German separates them:

NATIONALITY (noun): Ich bin Deutscher.
LANGUAGE (adjective): Ich spreche Deutsch.

The language is usually the adjective form:
Deutsch, Französisch, Englisch, Spanisch, Türkisch

But the nationality is a noun with gender:
Deutscher/Deutsche, Franzose/Französin, etc.

This is more complex than Dutch but gives you more precision!

steps.q: A woman says 'Ich bin Deutsche.' This means:
steps.hint: Ich bin = I am, Deutsche = German (female)

steps.title: I Live In... 🏡
steps.desc: Tell people where you live! Germans will want to know your city — and whether it's in the north or south.

steps.note: 'Wohnen' = to reside. Almost identical to Dutch 'wonen'! Conjugation: ich wohne, du wohnst, er/sie wohnt, wir wohnen.
Feminine (die). Plural: die Städte. The umlaut in the plural is typical of German!

steps.example: Ich wohne in einem kleinen Dorf.
steps.exampleEn: I live in a small village.
steps.note: Neuter (das). Plural: die Dörfer. Germany has thousands of beautiful Dörfer, especially in Bavaria and the Black Forest.

steps.exampleEn: In which street do you live?
steps.note: Feminine (die). The 'ß' (Eszett) is unique to German — you can also write 'Strasse'. Street names often end in '-straße', '-weg' (way), or '-allee'.

steps.title: 💬 Where do you live?
steps.text:
German small talk about where you live:

❓ Wo wohnst du? — Where do you live?
💬 Ich wohne in [city]. — I live in [city].
💬 Ich wohne in der Nähe von... — I live near...

Germans often follow up with:
❓ Gefällt es dir dort? — Do you like it there?
❓ Und was machst du beruflich? — And what do you do for work?

🏙️ Big German cities:
Berlin (capital, ~3.7M)
München / Munich (Bavaria, ~1.5M)
Hamburg (port city, ~1.9M)
Köln / Cologne (Rhine, ~1.1M)
Frankfurt (finance, ~750K)

steps.q: How do you say 'I live in Munich'?

steps.title: German Verb Conjugation Basics
steps.text:
German regular verbs follow a predictable pattern:

1. Take the infinitive: wohnen (to live)
2. Remove -en to get the stem: wohn
3. Add endings:

ich → stem + e (ich wohne)
du → stem + st (du wohnst)
er/sie → stem + t (er wohnt)
wir → stem + en (wir wohnen)
ihr → stem + t (ihr wohnt)
sie/Sie → stem + en (sie wohnen)

Almost identical to Dutch! The 'du' and 'ihr' forms are the main difference.

steps.title: Languages I Speak 🗣️
steps.desc: Germany dubs EVERYTHING — so they've had less English exposure than the Dutch! Learn to talk about languages.

steps.note: An irregular (strong) verb! ich spreche, du SPRICHST, er SPRICHT. The 'e' changes to 'i' in du/er forms. This is called a 'vowel change' verb.
Super useful! 'Bisschen' comes from 'Biss' (bite) — literally 'a little bite'. Similar to Dutch 'een beetje'.
Feminine (die). Plural: die Sprachen. 'Muttersprache' = mother tongue. Related to 'sprechen' (to speak).

steps.title: 🇩🇪 Germans and languages
steps.text:
Unlike the Dutch, most Germans grew up with DUBBED TV and movies. This means English skills vary a lot!

Useful phrases:
• Ich spreche Deutsch. — I speak German.
• Ich spreche ein bisschen Deutsch. — I speak a little German.
• Sprichst du Englisch? — Do you speak English?
• Ich lerne Deutsch. — I am learning German.
• Ich verstehe dich nicht. — I don't understand you.

Pro tip: In big cities (Berlin, Munich, Hamburg) many people speak English. In smaller towns and in the East, less so. Always try German first — it's greatly appreciated!

deepDive.title: Why German TV is dubbed
deepDive.text:
Germany, Austria, and Switzerland dub ALL foreign content — movies, TV series, everything.

This is a huge industry: Germany has world-class dubbing studios, and famous actors have a dedicated 'Synchronsprecher' (voice actor) who dubs all their roles.

Tom Hanks always sounds the same in German — because the same voice actor has dubbed him for 30+ years!

The downside: less casual English exposure compared to the Dutch or Scandinavians. The upside: German dubbing quality is considered the best in the world.

steps.q: How do you say 'I speak a little German'?

steps.title: Vowel Change Verbs
steps.text:
Some German verbs change their stem vowel in du/er/sie forms:

Sprechen (to speak): e → i
ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht

Other common ones:
• sehen (to see): du siehst, er sieht
• lesen (to read): du liest, er liest
• geben (to give): du gibst, er gibt
• nehmen (to take): du nimmst, er nimmt

Only the du and er/sie/es forms change. Wir/ihr/sie stay regular.

steps.title: All About Me 💫
steps.desc: Combine everything! Introduce yourself fully in German — name, origin, city, and languages.

steps.title: 🎯 Your German self-intro
steps.text:
Here's a full self-introduction pattern:

Hallo! Ich heiße [name]. — Hello! My name is [name].
Ich komme aus [country]. — I come from [country].
Ich wohne in [city]. — I live in [city].
Ich spreche [language] und ein bisschen Deutsch. — I speak [language] and a little German.
Freut mich! — Nice to meet you!

Example:
Hallo! Ich heiße Maria. — Hello! My name is Maria.
Ich komme aus Spanien. — I come from Spain.
Ich wohne in Berlin. — I live in Berlin.
Ich spreche Spanisch und ein bisschen Deutsch. — I speak Spanish and a little German.
Freut mich! — Nice to meet you!

steps.q: Someone says 'Wo wohnst du?' — you reply:
How do you say 'Nice to meet you' in German?
